Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay The Action; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Feb-07-2025 Continued To Mar-10-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Punitive Damages Allegations And Request For Punitive Damages In Plaintiff?S Complaint As To Defendant Christrian Alberto Figueroa
Off Calendar Feb-18-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CHRISTIAN FIGUEROA's Motion To Strike Punitive Damages Allegations And Request For Punitive Damages In Plaintiff'S Complaint As To Defendant Christian Alberto Figueroa.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Christian Alberto's motion to strike plaintiff Mary-Ann Kubal's punitive damages allegations is denied.

Kubal alleges that "she was an Uber passenger, riding home to her apartment in San Francisco, California. Her Uber driver was Defendant Christian Alberto Figueroa. Mr. Figueroa was driving under the influence of alcohol and/or drugs. He was operating the vehicle recklessly and crashed into a light pole, causing severe and life-threatening injuries to Mary-Ann Kubal, as set forth more fully below." (Compl. para. 1.)

Alberto contends that Kubal's factual showing is insufficient because it does not specify the alleged level of intoxication or other additional facts, such as a particular pattern of reckless driving, that would make punitive damages appropriate. To the extent Alberto claims Kubal's pleading is insufficiently specific, the court finds otherwise; Kubal is required to plead only the ultimate fact of intoxication and not the evidentiary facts that she may use at trial to support the ultimate fact. (See Clauson v. Superior Court (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 1253, 1255 ["In order to survive a motion to strike an allegation of punitive damages, the ultimate facts showing an entitlement to such relief must be pled by a plaintiff."].)

Alberto also contends that the ultimate fact of driving while intoxicated is insufficient to support punitive damages. This is incorrect; the Supreme Court held otherwise in Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 892-893. The case on which Alberto relies, Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82, 90, is not to the contrary; Dawes considered whether to apply the new categorical rule announced in Taylor retroactively but ultimately did not reach the question because it found that the allegations of the plaintiff before it were sufficient to state a claim for punitive damages even under pre-Taylor authority. In any event, Dawes (a decision of the California Court of Appeal) could not overrule the California Supreme Court's decision in Taylor.

Order signed.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
